description:
“TheJapanese bobis a sharp, sculpted chin-skimming cut which is defined by clean lines and a subtle inward curve which frames the face,” says hairstylist and salon owner Melissa Timperley. As well as having a precise, architectural quality to its shape, the style is often complete with a glassy, ultra-smooth finish.
Timperley adds that the overall effect is polished but effortless, giving it a contemporary feel. “In the salon, you’d ask for a blunt, Japanese-inspired bob with softly bevelled ends that naturally turn inwards. The key is precision: the stylist should keep the outline clean and structured while using internal shaping to create movement.”

trend_name: How does the bob incorporate Japanese styling?
description:
Detailed structure and intentional balance associate this bob with typical Japanese cutting techniques. “It’s largely a simplistic style which relies on the hair's natural texture and movement,” explains Andreas Wild, creative stylist ambassador at John Frieda. “Japanese cutting is not about fighting the hair, but about carving a shape that works with your natural shape.”
Timperley agrees, adding that the precision-focused Japanese approach (and the common use of sculptural silhouettes) is what creates a strong, graphic cut that maintains softness and movement within. “This is exactly what defines the Japanese bob.”

trend_name: Which hair types and face shapes are best suited to the Japanese bob?
description:
The Japanese bob is ideal for those with straight or slightly wavy hair, as these hair types will naturally complement the precision of the cut. “Fine hair also benefits from the blunt edge, as this creates the illusion of thickness,” continues Timperley. If weight is removed internally, thick hair can also appear “light and swingy” with a sleek bob.
Celebrity hairstylist and textured hair specialist Lorraine Dublin explains that opting for the Japanese bob is also a great choice for coily hair, but it’s important to take into account curl behavior, shrinkage and density. “On textured hair, the bob should feel precise, but it should move and live naturally within the curl pattern. Coils can shrink up to 50–80%, so the cut has to be tailored dry, allowing for that movement.”
Rather than forcing sleekness, stylists will focus on sculpting the curl with intention. “When done correctly, a bob on coily hair looks powerful, defined and full of personality,” adds Dublin.
As for face shape, blunt styles are especially flattering on oval, heart and round faces (by helping to sharpen and lift facial features), but adapting the cut to your unique shape is key. “It's about tailoring, not copying,” advises Cusick. “If someone has a rounder face, I might leave it slightly longer at the front to elongate the silhouette.”
Wild adds: “While it's a more extreme style of bob compared to softer, layered variations, its boldness is part of its appeal. It’s particularly flattering for those with a defined jawline, as it’s designed to sit sharply and flat against it, really drawing attention to the structure of the face and neck.”

trend_name: What are some things to consider when opting for a Japanese bob?
description:
Unlike its more relaxed and lived-in counterparts (such as feathered and shaggy bobs), Wild stresses that the Japanese bob isn’t a style to hide behind. “Its flat, straight nature inherently emphasizes the face, bringing all attention to your jawline and neck.” For those who prefer softer, low-effort styles, consider carefully; Wild notes that the Japanese bob requires a level of commitment to styling (to maintain sleekness), and regular maintenance.
According to Timperley, it’s also advisable to avoid heavy external layering as this can disrupt the desired silhouette. “Choosing a stylist who is experienced in structured bobs is essential, as this is a precision cut.”

trend_name: How would I approach styling and maintenance?
description:
Again, commitment is key—and this will mean regular trims, every four to six weeks. Cusick warns that if a Japanese bob is left to grow out, it will quickly lose its crisp, blunt effect. “This is a haircut that looks best when it's freshly cut; it rewards you for keeping on top of it.”
Day-to-day styling to keep the bob sleek and straight is easy, but visibly elevates the cut. “Blow drying with a paddle brush will help to encourage the inward curve, while using straighteners on the ends can refine the bevel,” suggests Timperley.
“However, always protect the hair with a heat protectant,” adds Wild. A lightweight protectant spray or hair serum will not only enhance shine and achieve a reflective, glass-like finish, but will further reduce frizz. “For those tiny, inevitable flyaways at the root or around the hairline, a small amount can work wonders to smooth them down for a perfectly polished look.”

Whether you have sensitive skin, hair that grows too fast, or frequent salon visits, the best bikini trimmers are great at-home maintenance in a pinch. Although they may make your hair grow back a bit quicker, you’ll never have to worry about nicks, cuts, or painful ingrowns. After consulting the experts and reviewing numerous customer reviews, we present the eight best bikini trimmers for a simpler, less painful way to keep the area under control.

trend_name: Kaia Gerber Debuts a Vintage Lob That Channels Cindy Crawford
description:
Between the ’90s supermodel blow-dry and the iconic feathered cut, the pair has truly never looked more alike.
It’s official:Kaia Gerberhas never looked more like her iconic supermodel mother, Cindy Crawford. The 24-year-old model and actress unveiled an unexpectedlayered lobtransformation, which we’ve dubbed “The Cindy,” at last night’s Academy Museum Fifth Annual Gala, and it’s safe to say it was the look of the evening. Think identical length and a razored finish reminiscent of her mother’s effortlessly chic late’90s bob, complete with a high-shine modern allure.
“We love the ’90s bob with its soft, point-cut perimeter and face-framing shape of varied lengths to enhance suitability and complement different lifestyles,” says Larry King senior stylist Steve Braithwaite. “It looks fantastic with a side parting that lifts up and away from the face, enhancing and bringing out the eyes and cheekbones.”
If you’re looking for a silky, versatile update to refresh your lengths this fall (without committing to a bold, new bob), this is the voluminous, flatteringlob haircutfor you.
While the look is hardly a dramatic departure from Gerber’s signature beauty aesthetic, it’s undoubtedly one of the model’s shortest crops in recent years. The new haircut falls to a collarbone-grazing length, with softly shattered, face-framing ends, similar to Jennifer Aniston’s famous ’90s style but with a luxurious, glossed finish.
The best part of The Cindy? The elegant, vintage-inspired cut offers a range of effortless styling options, from glossy, red-carpet looks to breezy everyday styles.
“Smooth it out for an elegant finish or rough it up with mousse and spray for a casual, trendy vibe,” advises Braithwaite. “The haircut is so versatile that I’m sure it will be just as popular this time around as it was the first time.”
Whether you’re a fan of air-drying your natural texture, or bouncy modern blowouts, there’s a version of this iconic style for everyone.
Gerber has been channeling her mother’s signature hairstyles frequently in recent years, including her soft beehive at the Palm Royale world premiere and her bold Bardot ’do in St. Moritz. And while her odes to her mother’s iconic looks are plentiful, we can firmly state that this healthy crop is our favorite; not to mention it’s perfectly on-trend for the fall season.

trend_name: Who Is the Feathered Bob Best For?
description:
Hailey Bieber is the latest adopter of this hairstyle, showcasing her shoulder-length brunette hair with gently feathered ends. “The feathered bob has movement and due to its cutting techniques, it allows your natural hair texture to shine while creating a beautiful silhouette,” says Hawkins.
When it comes to tailoring the cut, Hawkins says that it is about “the distribution of weight within your haircut.” A thorough consultation, as always, is key, as there are multiple ways of ensuring your resulting style looks balanced and brings out the best in your natural texture.

trend_name: The Feathered Bob vs. the French Bob
description: Both have a lived-in, relaxed feel, so it is easy to confuse the two. But generally, the feathered style is significantly lighter. “It’s more intentional. The weight is more directed at the root, leaving a lot more softness throughout the length and ends,” Hawkins tells ELLE.

trend_name: How to Style the Feathered Bob
description: As one of the more low-key, understated cuts, your daily styling routine should be relatively easy. If air-drying is your preferred method, run a leave-in conditioning treatment to minimize frizz and encourage shine. You can also use a diffuser to restore definition to curls, or use a hot brush for a smoother effect.

trend_name: The difference between fine hair and thin hair
description: It is much simpler than we think. “Fine hairrefers to the thickness of each individual strand, while thin hair relates to the density (the number of strands on your scalp),” says McKnight. “You don’t necessarily have both. Many people have fine hair but a lot of it, for example. Conversely, you may havethin hair, but the diameter of each strand could be medium to thick, with any texture from straight and wavy to curly.”

trend_name: Is your hair fine or thin?
description:
McKnight has an easy solution. “To work out if your hair is fine, take a single strand and roll it between your thumb and finger,” says McKnight. “If you can barely feel it, your hair is fine. If you can feel it slightly, your hair is medium. Thick hair can be felt easily.”
He explains that hair density, on the other hand, refers to the number of individual strands on your scalp. “To test this, tie your hair into a ponytail and measure its circumference with your forefinger and thumb. A ponytail under two inches in diameter is considered low density, between two and four inches is medium, and anything over four inches is high density.”
In case you’re wondering whether these two factors can be true at once—yes, you can have both fine and thin hair. For example, if your strands are small in diameter and you don’t have many of them, you likely have a combination of both.

trend_name: The best haircuts for fine or thin hair
description:
When it comes to the best hairstyles for both fine and thin hair, McKnight states that there’s no perfect rule book.
“It all depends on your personal style, how your roots grow, and whether you’re a heat-styling devotee or more of a wash-and-go person,” he says. “Your hair stylist will also take your hair texture and type into account. There are many factors that come into play when creating the right haircut, and what works for one person doesn’t necessarily work for the next.”
Giving a one-size-fits-all answer to the “perfect cut” is a disservice to both you and your stylist. However, there are some key factors worth considering before you brave any sort of chop.
“If volume is key, consider shorter lengths,” he suggests. “It’s much harder to achieve and maintain lift when your hair is halfway down your back and weighed down by its own length. Whether it’s adding layers, shorter pieces around the face, or keeping the ends blunt, this is where your hairdresser’s expertise really comes into play.”

trend_name: Expert tips for styling fine hair
description: Here, McKnight outlines his best advice for giving fine hair body and shape that lasts.

trend_name: Expert tips for styling thin hair
description: For thin hair, the same principles apply, but you might need a little extra help with hold.

trend_name: The French Pin Is Replacing the Claw Clip—And It’s the Best Style for Fine Hair Types
description:
This unassuming accessory is a chic game-changer for wispy strands.
Every item on this page was chosen by anELLEeditor. We may earn commission on some of the items you choose to buy.
Everyone and their sister might be gettinga bob, but the most game-changing thing in my beauty repertoire right now is the humble French pin.
The genius lies in its simplicity. This sleek, U-shaped accessory essentially holds a chignon in place and lies flat against the head, which makes for a much more comfortable wear than aclaw clip—especially if you, like me, regularly lean against the subway windows on your commute. It’s also a more elevated alternative to a scrunchie, which tends to make my fine, low-density lengths appear much thinner when swallowed by a chunky hair tie.
While the French pin is currently taking over social media, my earliest introduction to it was more than two decades ago. My stepmother was a flight attendant, and these pins were a necessity to keep her airline-mandated chignon in place through long hours while requiring minimal styling time (she was trained to do her hair in less than five minutes).
The pin’s history goes even further, with wood and bone versions dating back to ancient times. Its use is not reserved for the French either, as various iterations are worn across the globe.
Right now, the appeal of the French pin is likely driven by the penchant for polished yet understated beauty routines, which a chignon fits perfectly into. “French pins are gaining popularity as they are a more elegant alternative to a claw clip,” agrees hairstylistHollie Rose Clarke. Plus, the discreet nature of the French pin feels refined enough for black-tie occasions compared to a claw clip or a scrunchie.
It’s also an excellent way to disguise second- and third-day hair. “It’s very connected to the fact that most French women wash their hair only once a week. On the last few days of the week, they pin it up and that’s how they make their hair look good,” says French hairstylistDavid Mallett, adding that it’s a way of “making undone hair look great.”
Plus, as celebrities gravitate towards retro-glamorous looks on the red carpet (thinkRayeand her signature bouncy, curly bob andPamela Anderson’s updo at theGolden Globes), there is a clear shift towards timeless hair.
It’s a style that I’ve always strived for, and I’m drawn to the nostalgic, ’90s feel of the French pin chignon. VeryRachel Greenmeets Catherine Deneuve.
Using the French pin is fairly easy; a breeze once you’ve found what works for you. Clarke shares three steps to achieve it:
Alternatively, you can also twist your hair into a regular bun and pierce the pin through it, making sure that it goes through both the bun and the hair close to your scalp.
The kind of pin you use matters, as I’ve realized after trialing multiple materials and sizes. Onlonger bobsto medium-length hair, stick to a light version that’s no longer than the length of an iPhone. Heavy, metal pins tend to fall off due to the extra weight.Thicker, longer hairmight suit a slightly larger pin.
I’m never without myLa Bonne Brosse pin, which has the perfect curve that holds on to fine, slippery hair. Mallett’s own pin works especially well when you’re delaying your nextshampoo. You can also prep your locks with a texture spray for extra grip, if needed.
Clarke is also a fan of the ones from Kitsch andFiona Franchimon No. 1 Hairpin, which is a set of three. “They help hold the hair in place all day without causing any snagging or damage. These are a great starting place as they are smaller and the unique design holds the hair by creating a pinch point, similar to a bobby pin but bigger,” Clarke tells ELLE.
Considering how it combines the practicality of keeping my hair up while making me look pulled together, it’s safe to say that I’ve made the French pin my entire personality. I promise, once you get the hang of twisting your hair up and the accessory in—you’ll wonder where it’s been all your life.

trend_name: The Most Striking Blonde Hairstyles of the Year
description:
The light shade is more versatile than you think.
Whether natural or chosen, there’s continuous proof of the age-old saying that blondes have more fun. Case in point: the diverse roster of celebrities who are currently wearing the hair color. For anyone who wasn’t born a blonde, it’s certainly a bold choice for those who decides to dabble in the bleached look. Still, it undoubtedly commands attention on a red carpet, makes a short pixie cut stand out, or perfectly punctuates a major style transition, such as a big chop. Blonde hair does require more—if not the most—maintenance than other hair colors, but it’s also versatile and looks good on long, short, curly, or straight hair. A welcomed shift away from the restrictive notion that exposed darker roots signal unkemptness has made way for ombré and more natural-looking highlights to take the spotlight for blonde hair. You’ll see from this list of celebrities with blonde hair that, in all its variations, blonde, whether born or bleached, is the undisputed way to make a statement with hair color.
Kristen Bell hovers in between blonde and brown with ombré ashy blonde ends and soft brown roots.
On the opposite end of the brightness scale, singer and actress Ana Mena opts for full platinum locks with peekaboo darker roots to match her brows.
Natalia Dyer’s glossy, long, perfectly blended blonde tresses are a case study in seamlessly merging darker brown roots with golden highlights and hints of strawberry blonde.
Very few styles can rival the boldness of Jerrie Johnson’s platinum pixie. If you’re up for the challenge, consider carving a part into the near-buzz cut.
Darker lowlights, painted around the crown, keep Sydney Sweeney’s golden locks from looking too one-dimensional.
A wig is the perfect opportunity to try a blonde hairstyle without the commitment or potential damage of dyeing your natural hair.
Ease into the light shade by going for a warmer tone of blonde with hints of strawberry, similar to Addison Rae’s.
To keep skin from looking washed out, warmth and dimension with slightly deeper shades of blonde are essential for fairer skin, like Dakota Fanning’s.
Ciara went for more strategic highlights, ombré roots, and a mix of both warm and ashy undertones to make the lighter tone blend seamlessly with her caramel skin.
Paris Hilton’s cascading blonde curls get a more modern touch with curtain bangs.
Emma Chamberlain looks like she stepped right out of a ’90s time capsule with this platinum pixie. The throwback markers being the longer sideburns and piecy bangs.
We’ve waxed poetic about Naomi Watts’s perfectly styled blonde bob. What’s most impressive is how versatile she’s shown the shorter cut and color to be.
Kate Hudson’s sleek, wavy, perfectly ombré style is giving mermaid in all the right ways.
Brie Larson chose a cooler ash-blonde pixie that contrasts beautifully with her warmer skin and vibrant yellow top.
The slightly reddish undertones in Margot Robbie’s locks keep it from looking like a too-buttery Barbie blonde.
It’s nearly impossible to picture Queen Bey wearing anything other than this warm blonde hair color and big barrel curls.
Amanda Seyfried’s darker crown and lighter ends offer a subtle alternative to more dramatic ombré shading.
Bangs on blonde hair can look a bit babydoll-ish, but Jennifer Lawrence pushes them into grown-up territory with well-blended highlights and light layers.
Daenerys Targaryen may have made this level of platinum iconic years ago, but Beatrice Puccilli shows it’s timeless.
Some celebrities veer back and forth between light and dark hair, but Michelle Williams has stayed true to her blonde roots and short styles, proving that when you find what works for you, stick with it.

trend_name: Why Butterfly Haircuts With Bangs Are 2026’s Go-to Look
description:
The style works on every hair length.
Your fall wardrobe isn’t the only place to lean into fringe this season. Now that balmy, hair-sticking-to-skin weather has come and gone, you can comfortably try a hairstyle withbangs. One of the trendier bang hairstyles that works on both short and long hair is the butterfly haircut with bangs.
The style is a hybrid of looks and somewhat of a 1970s throwback that’s been updated with a bang. Butterfly haircuts quite literally look like the wings of a butterfly, fanned out to frame the face. The look can be achieved through either acutor with layered curls. While many iterations of the style have been seen on longer hair, it’s doable on hair that’s bob-length, too. Adding a straight, curly, orcurtain bangmodernizes the style from the Farrah Fawcett version that popularized it. Whether your hair is already cut into layers and you’re curious to try the style, or you want to add some dimension with a fresh cut, browse the variations of the butterfly haircut with bangs below for inspiration.
Blackpink’s Lisa perfectly nails the butterfly with bangs look on softly highlighted hair. Keeping the bangs smooth adds a nice contrast to the wide waves.
The butterfly flips are less obvious on dark-toned hair, but add nice texture to lob-length hair.
If your fringe has begun to grow out, easily recreate the flips that make this style by shaping them up and outward using a curling iron or small round brush.
And let’s not forget about the curly girls. Experiment with your natural texture to create wing-like flips around the face.
Parted down the middle, these curtain bangs receive a sleek edge that defines the moment.
Another, more subtle option is to keep the crown and blunt bangs very sleek and focus the flips toward the ends of longer locks.
The style works on straighter hair, too. Keep the bangs on the longer side and work the wing texture into the hair along the cheekbones and shoulders.
For a more defined variation, pair structured curtain bangs with tighter, shoulder-length curls.
Sabrina Carpenter has worn several variations of the butterfly haircut with bangs. Here, she shows off slept-in curls at the back, which puts the winged front in the spotlight.
The style takes on more of a wet look with slicked curtain bangs and deep bends along the sides of your face.
Whether it’s a grown-out pixie cut or a layered bob, swoop the pieces of hair near your temples and ears toward the back for a winged effect.
Use a 1-1.5-inch curling iron to coax the hair framing your face into bends right above the brow. Take a waver or crimping iron to the rest to finish off this look.
Super loose waves, focused to the ends, are a noncommital entry point to this style.
Curling the hair makes the winged portions more pronounced, but as you can see here, a fluffy texture and the right haircut naturally creates the style, too.
Bring the texture in with the cut instead of the curls, like this perfectly layered variety that effortlessly bends into the butterfly look as she walks.
Once the cut is set, you can make the style as casual or formal as you want by pinning it up in the back or wearing it all hanging down.
Butterfly hair lends itself to more pronounced flips, but you can always ease into the trend with a looser and softer version like this.
